headline: Coffee Market Brief — 22 April 2026
key_takeaways: Coffee futures (July contract) fell to $287.80 US cents/lb, breaking below the previously expected $2.90-$3.05 range.
Roaster coverage is at better levels than last year, contributing to a quieter market despite past volatility.
The market is expected to test $2.75/lb this week, with potential for a further 10-cent drop if breached.
risks: Potential for nearby ICE-certified inventory levels to remain tight until the Brazilian crop 26/27.
Possibility of increased coffee coming to the exchange for May, offsetting nearby certified demand.
Breaching the $2.75/lb support level could lead to a significant price decline.
roaster_actions: Monitor nearby ICE-certified inventory levels closely.
Evaluate opportunities to fix nearby futures contracts on any further selloffs.
Be prepared for potential price volatility if the $2.75/lb level is breached.
Consider hedging strategies based on the expected price range of $2.80-$2.95 if $2.75 holds.
raw_report: This week, coffee futures prices basis the most active July contract fell 8.5 cents to finish at $287.80 US cents/lb. I wrote last week that I expected the market to trade in a narrower $2.90 to $3.05 range as resolution of the conflict with Iran determined the price action. Indeed, it seems that the conflict with Iran seems to be (maybe, kinda, sorta nearby) resolved, so the market had tested below the defined range after moving up to a high of $3 on Tuesday. Therefore, my market prediction of a relatively quiet period leading up to May first notice day proved correct. I believe the main reason why the market has stayed quiet during this period (the same one that has seen an immense amount of volatility in 2025) is that roaster coverage is at much better levels now than a year ago. Last year, roasters were forced to fix futures prices at higher and higher levels as they ran out of time approaching contract expiry. This year, the market has been steady to lower, allowing roasters plenty of opportunities to fix their nearby futures contracts on selloffs. This week, as we transition into the May contract delivery period, it will be interesting to see whether nearby demand for ICE-certified inventories continues. If it does, it could mean that nearby inventory levels at the destination will continue to stay tight until the Brazilian crop 26/27 starts flowing. However, it is possible that more coffee will come to the exchange for May, offsetting the nearby certified demand. In all, I would expect the market to try to test $2.75/lb this week and break down 10 cents lower if that level is breached, or continue in the same weekly range of $2.80-$2.95 if it holds.
